bytes.topic: Why Queues — decoupling, async processing, load leveling
bytes.title: NATS JetStream — Decoupling, Async Processing, and Load Leveling Done Right
bytes.body:
Without a queue, your API handler is directly coupled to every downstream operation — if email sending spikes latency, your HTTP response does too. NATS JetStream breaks this with persistent, replay-capable subjects. Publish once to a subject like `orders.created`; multiple consumer groups pull at their own pace without blocking the publisher.

Load leveling in NATS uses push or pull consumers. Pull consumers (preferred for workers) let each instance call `Fetch(batch)` only when ready, preventing fast publishers from overwhelming slow processors. A durable consumer with `DeliverPolicy: DeliverAllPolicy` guarantees no message is lost across restarts.

Critically, NATS uses per-subject acknowledgment — `msg.Ack()`, `msg.Nak()`, or `msg.NakWithDelay(d)` for retry backoff. A Nak requeues immediately; `NakWithDelay` implements exponential backoff without external tooling. Combined with `MaxDeliver` on the stream, you get dead-letter semantics natively. This lets API handlers return 202 Accepted instantly while workers drain the subject at sustainable throughput.

bytes.topic: Why Caching — reducing DB load, latency, cache-aside pattern
bytes.title: NATS JetStream KV — Cache-Aside Pattern Without Adding Redis
bytes.body:
NATS JetStream's built-in Key-Value store gives you a distributed cache without bolting on Redis or Memcached. Every KV bucket is backed by a JetStream stream — replicated, persisted, and TTL-aware by default.

The cache-aside pattern maps directly: call `kv.Get(ctx, key)`; if `jetstream.ErrKeyNotFound` is returned, fetch from Postgres, marshal the result, and `kv.Put(ctx, key, data)`. The bucket TTL handles expiry automatically — no manual cleanup jobs.

Where this pays off: high-read, low-write entities like user profiles, feature flags, and lookup tables. A 5-minute TTL on a `user-cache` bucket routinely cuts Postgres read traffic by 80%+ under normal fan-out patterns. NATS serves the cached bytes in-memory from the same connection your services already hold open.

Two sharp edges: TTL is bucket-scoped, not per-key — use separate buckets for mixed expiry needs. For write-through invalidation, call `kv.Delete(ctx, key)` before committing to the DB; skipping this risks returning stale data on the next read within the TTL window.
bytes.code_snippet:
kv, _ := js.CreateOrUpdateKeyValue(ctx, jetstream.KeyValueConfig{
	Bucket:   "user-cache",
	TTL:      5 * time.Minute,
	Replicas: 1,
})

entry, err := kv.Get(ctx, userID)
if errors.Is(err, jetstream.ErrKeyNotFound) {
	user, _ := db.GetUser(ctx, userID)
	data, _ := json.Marshal(user)
	kv.Put(ctx, userID, data)
	return user
}
var user User
json.Unmarshal(entry.Value(), &user)
return user

bytes.topic: Message Delivery Guarantees — at-least-once vs at-most-once vs exactly-once
bytes.title: NATS Message Delivery Guarantees — At-Most-Once, At-Least-Once, and Exactly-Once with JetStream
bytes.body:
Core NATS is fire-and-forget: if a subscriber is offline or slow, messages are dropped — that's at-most-once. No persistence, no retries.

JetStream changes the equation. Configure a durable consumer with `AckPolicy: nats.AckExplicitPolicy` and unacknowledged messages are redelivered after `AckWait` expires — giving you at-least-once. Your handler must be idempotent; duplicate delivery is expected on crash or timeout. Cap retries with `MaxDeliver`.

Exactly-once requires two coordinated halves. On the publisher side, set the `Nats-Msg-Id` header — JetStream deduplicates within the stream's `Duplicates` window (default 2 minutes). On the consumer side, swap `msg.Ack()` for `msg.AckSync()`, which blocks until the server confirms receipt of the ack. Skipping either half gives you at-least-once, not exactly-once.

One critical caveat: the dedup window is finite. A publisher outage longer than the `Duplicates` duration reopens the duplicate risk, so tune it to your worst-case downtime.

bytes.topic: Idempotency — designing consumers to safely handle duplicate messages
bytes.title: NATS JetStream Idempotency — Safe Duplicate Handling in Consumers
bytes.body:
NATS JetStream's publish-side deduplication (via the `Nats-Msg-Id` header and the stream's `DuplicateWindow`) only catches duplicates at ingestion time — once a message is in the stream, consumers are on their own. With `AckExplicit` delivery policy, any unacked or `Nak()`'d message is redelivered, making consumer-side idempotency essential.

The pattern: require publishers to set `Nats-Msg-Id` as a message header (JetStream persists it on the message metadata). In the consumer, perform an atomic check-and-set — using Redis `SET NX` or a database upsert — against that ID before processing. If the key already exists, the message was already handled: call `msg.Ack()` to drain it from the pending set without reprocessing.

One subtle trap: if processing succeeds but `msg.Ack()` fails, the message redelivers. Your idempotency store prevents double execution, but you still need `Ack()` to succeed — never delete the idempotency key on success. Retain it for at least your stream's `MaxAge` plus redelivery window.

With `nats.MaxDeliver(5)` on the consumer binding, unresolvable failures are automatically dead-lettered after five attempts, keeping your pending count clean.

bytes.topic: Dead Letter Queues — poison message handling, retry strategies with backoff
bytes.title: NATS JetStream Dead Letter Queues — Poison Message Handling and Backoff Strategies
bytes.body:
NATS JetStream has no native DLQ, but the pattern composes cleanly. Set MaxDeliver on your consumer to cap total attempts. Once exhausted, JetStream emits an advisory to $JS.EVENT.ADVISORY.CONSUMER.MAX_DELIVERIES.<stream>.<consumer> — subscribe there to capture the original stream sequence, then republish to a dedicated DEAD.> stream for post-mortem inspection.

For backoff, skip manual NakWithDelay math. NATS 2.9+ supports BackOff []time.Duration directly on ConsumerConfig — declare your full retry schedule upfront and JetStream handles redelivery timing without application-side logic. [5s, 30s, 2m] means first retry after 5s, second after 30s, third after 2m. MaxDeliver should equal len(BackOff) + 1.

If you need runtime flexibility, call msg.NakWithDelay(delay) where delay derives from meta.NumDelivered via msg.Metadata(). Note: AckWait acts as a floor — if your backoff delay is shorter than AckWait, the server requeues at AckWait expiry instead.

Bind your DLQ stream to DEAD.> with a long MaxAge and MaxDeliver: 1 — preserve these messages for replay, never re-process them automatically.

bytes.topic: Redis as Cache — TTL, eviction policies, cache-aside vs write-through vs write-behind
bytes.title: NATS + Redis — Cache-Aside, Write-Through, and Eviction in Message-Driven Services
bytes.body:
When NATS queue subscribers hit a database on every message, latency compounds fast. Redis slots naturally between your NATS handler and Postgres. Cache-aside is the default: on a cache miss, the handler fetches from DB, writes to Redis with TTL, and continues. TTL must be shorter than your NATS stream's MaxAge — stale cache entries that outlive message retention cause silent inconsistencies.

Write-through suits idempotent NATS handlers: write to Redis and DB atomically before ACKing the message. If the subscriber crashes post-write but pre-ACK, NATS redelivers; Redis deduplicates via the cached key.

Write-behind is riskier: the handler updates Redis immediately, ACKs, then publishes a secondary subject (`db.sync.*`) consumed by a durable JetStream consumer that flushes to Postgres asynchronously. You get lower p99 latency but accept a data-loss window if the sync consumer lags.

For eviction, prefer `allkeys-lru` in read-heavy NATS fan-out topologies. Use `volatile-ttl` when only TTL-tagged keys are cache-eligible. Avoid `noeviction` entirely — NATS redelivery storms under backpressure will OOM your Redis node fast.

bytes.topic: Redis as Queue — pub/sub vs streams vs lists, persistence trade-offs
bytes.title: NATS JetStream vs Core Pub/Sub — Picking the Right Queue Primitive
bytes.body:
NATS gives you three distinct queuing models with different durability guarantees. Core pub/sub is fire-and-forget — messages vanish if no subscriber is connected. Zero persistence, at-most-once delivery. Useful for real-time telemetry where stale data is worthless, not for jobs.

JetStream Streams flip the model: messages persist to disk or memory and replay on demand. Consumers can be push-based (server delivers) or pull-based (worker polls), with configurable ack policies — AckExplicit, AckAll, or AckNone. This is your durable, ordered, replayable channel — analogous to Redis Streams.

For task queues specifically, use JetStream with WorkQueuePolicy retention: each message is delivered to exactly one consumer in the group, then deleted on ack. Unlike Core queue groups — which silently drop unacked messages on subscriber crash — JetStream redelivers after AckWait expires, up to MaxDeliver attempts, then routes to a dead-letter subject.

Persistence trade-off is explicit: FileStorage survives restarts, MemoryStorage is faster but volatile. For critical workloads, pair FileStorage with Replicas: 3 on a clustered NATS server to survive a node loss without data loss.

bytes.topic: Kafka — partitions, consumer groups, offset management, log compaction
bytes.title: NATS JetStream vs Kafka — Partitions, Consumer Groups, and Log Compaction Mapped
bytes.body:
Kafka partitions, consumer groups, and log compaction all have NATS JetStream equivalents — but the mental model shifts significantly.

Instead of partitions, NATS uses subject hierarchies for sharding: route `orders.us.*` and `orders.eu.*` to separate filtered stream consumers, achieving parallel processing without explicit partition assignment.

Consumer groups map to queue subscriptions on a durable consumer. Multiple subscribers sharing the same `QueueGroup` name and `Durable` name compete for messages — JetStream distributes work and tracks ack state per-consumer, not per-partition.

Offset management uses sequence numbers (uint64, per-stream). `DeliverByStartSequence` lets you seek to any point in the log. `AckPolicy: explicit` gives at-least-once delivery; use `nats.StartSequence(n)` on re-subscribe to resume from a known checkpoint without storing offsets externally.

Log compaction maps directly to `MaxMsgsPerSubject: 1` on a stream — JetStream retains only the latest message per subject key, purging older ones. This is exactly how the built-in Key-Value store (`js.CreateKeyValue`) works under the hood: a stream with compaction semantics and a structured subject scheme baked in.

bytes.topic: RabbitMQ — exchanges, routing keys, acknowledgements, prefetch count
bytes.title: NATS JetStream vs RabbitMQ — Exchanges, Acks, and Prefetch Translated
bytes.body:
NATS JetStream replaces RabbitMQ's exchange/routing-key model with subject-based routing. There are no exchange types — subjects like `orders.us.placed` serve as both address and filter. A Stream captures messages matching a pattern (`orders.>`), and a Consumer narrows that with `FilterSubject`. The `*` wildcard matches one token; `>` matches the rest of the path — equivalent to a topic exchange binding.

For acknowledgements, JetStream consumers use `AckExplicit` by default. Call `msg.Ack()` to confirm, `msg.Nak()` to requeue (or `msg.NakWithDelay(d)` for backoff), `msg.Term()` to dead-letter without requeue, and `msg.InProgress()` to reset the `AckWait` timer mid-processing. No separate DLX wiring needed — dead-lettering is per-message.

Prefetch count maps directly to `MaxAckPending` on the consumer config. Setting it to 10 means at most 10 unacked messages are in-flight per consumer at once. For pull consumers, `Fetch(n)` is naturally bounded by `MaxAckPending`, giving you explicit backpressure without a separate QoS call. Unlike RabbitMQ's channel-level `basic.qos`, this is scoped per consumer — no shared state across workers.

bytes.topic: BullMQ — job priorities, rate limiting, repeatable jobs, job dependencies
bytes.title: NATS JetStream — BullMQ Patterns: Priorities, Rate Limits, Repeatable Jobs, and Dependency Chains
bytes.body:
NATS JetStream has no BullMQ primitives, but you can replicate all four patterns natively. For job priorities, model them as separate subjects — `jobs.high`, `jobs.normal`, `jobs.low` — bound to a single stream. Spin up consumers that drain `jobs.high` first; subject-level filtering gives you the ordering without a priority queue abstraction.

Rate limiting maps directly to `MaxAckPending` on a push or pull consumer. Set it to your desired concurrency ceiling — JetStream will stop delivering new messages once that many are unacknowledged, giving you backpressure without a token bucket.

Repeatable jobs have no native scheduler in NATS, but a simple goroutine ticker that calls `js.Publish` on each interval is idiomatic and resilient — pair it with a KV store flag to make it single-leader across replicas.

Dependency chains work via ack-then-publish: after a worker calls `msg.Ack()`, it publishes the dependent job to the next subject. Because JetStream delivery is durable, the downstream job is guaranteed even if the producer crashes mid-flight — the dependent message is already persisted before the parent ack is sent.

bytes.topic: Celery — task routing, beat scheduler, result backends, task chaining
bytes.title: NATS JetStream as a Celery Alternative — Routing, Results, and Chaining
bytes.body: Celery abstractions map cleanly onto NATS JetStream primitives — without a separate broker process. For task routing, model priority lanes as subject hierarchies: `tasks.high.*`, `tasks.low.*`. Bind queue-group consumers per lane so workers pull only their tier. For a result backend, skip Redis entirely — JetStream's Key-Value store gives you durable, TTL-scoped result storage with `js.create_key_value(bucket='task_results')`. Write the result under the message's Nats-Msg-Id; readers poll or watch the bucket. Task chaining works via reply subjects: the producer sets `reply_to='tasks.report'` on the initial publish, and the email worker republishes its output to that subject on completion — zero coordinator needed. For beat-style scheduling, NATS lacks a native cron trigger, so pair a lightweight APScheduler process that calls `js.publish()` on interval — the JetStream stream absorbs bursts and provides deduplication via `Nats-Msg-Id` headers, preventing double-execution on scheduler restart.
bytes.code_snippet:
import asyncio, nats
from nats.js.api import StreamConfig, KeyValueConfig

async def main():
    nc = await nats.connect()
    js = nc.jetstream()

    await js.add_stream(StreamConfig(name="TASKS", subjects=["tasks.>"]))
    kv = await js.create_key_value(KeyValueConfig(bucket="task_results", ttl=3600))

    # Route high-priority task; chain output to tasks.report
    await js.publish("tasks.high.email", b'{"user":"alice"}',
                     headers={"Nats-Msg-Id": "t-001"},
                     stream="TASKS")

    # Worker writes result to KV (result backend)
    await kv.put("t-001", b'{"status":"sent"}')

    # Chained consumer reads tasks.report triggered by reply_to
    sub = await js.subscribe("tasks.report", queue="report-workers")
    msg = await sub.next_msg(timeout=5)
    await msg.ack()

asyncio.run(main())

bytes.topic: NATS — JetStream, subject-based routing, at-least-once delivery
bytes.title: NATS JetStream — Subject Routing and At-Least-Once Delivery Without the Guesswork
bytes.body:
JetStream turns NATS from a fire-and-forget bus into a durable messaging layer. Streams capture messages published to subject wildcards — define a stream on `orders.>` and every message to `orders.created`, `orders.paid`, or `orders.cancelled` is persisted. Consumers then pull or push from that stream with configurable AckPolicy.

For at-least-once delivery, set `AckPolicy: nats.AckExplicitPolicy` on your consumer. Each delivered message must be acknowledged with `msg.Ack()` within the `AckWait` window (default 30s). Unacked messages are redelivered up to `MaxDeliver` times. Use `msg.Nak()` to signal failure and trigger immediate redelivery, or `msg.NakWithDelay(5 * time.Second)` for backoff.

Subject-based routing is the key architectural lever: fan-out by publishing to `events.user.signup` and binding multiple consumers across different streams to overlapping wildcards. Unlike Kafka partitions, NATS subject hierarchy is free-form — no pre-declared topology needed. Combine with `FilterSubject` on a consumer to scope a single stream into independent processing lanes without duplicating storage.

bytes.topic: Backpressure — what happens when consumers lag, preventing memory blowout
bytes.title: NATS JetStream Backpressure — Keeping Lagging Consumers From Blowing Up Memory
bytes.body:
NATS JetStream has two consumer modes, and backpressure behaves differently in each. With push consumers, a lagging subscriber causes the server to keep buffering undelivered messages on the client side — your process heap grows until you OOM. The fix is `MaxAckPending` on the consumer config: once in-flight unacked messages hit that ceiling, JetStream pauses delivery automatically until ACKs drain it back down.

Pull consumers are inherently backpressure-safe. `cons.Fetch(n)` is a demand-driven request — the server sends exactly `n` messages and stops. Your processing loop becomes the throttle; memory stays bounded by definition.

For push consumers under sustained lag, pair `MaxAckPending` with a tight `AckWait`. Without it, timed-out messages redeliver on top of new ones, compounding pressure. `MaxDeliver` caps retry storms from poison messages.

For production workloads, pull consumers with a fixed `Fetch` window are the idiomatic NATS pattern. You get bounded memory, natural flow control, and real-time consumer health via `cons.Info()` — `NumPending` and `NumAckPending` tell you exactly where lag is building before it becomes a crisis.

bytes.topic: Ordering — when order matters, partitioning strategy for ordered processing
bytes.title: NATS JetStream Ordering — Subject-Based Partitioning for Guaranteed Sequence
bytes.body:
NATS JetStream doesn't expose numeric partitions like Kafka — ordering is per-subject. To process events in order, publish to a subject hierarchy keyed on a stable partition token, then bind one ordered consumer per partition.

Partition by hashing the entity key (user ID, order ID) modulo N and publishing to `events.p{n}`. JetStream's ordered consumer delivers messages in stream sequence within its `FilterSubjects`, with `MaxDeliver: 1` enforced automatically — no gaps, no duplicates.

Spin up N goroutines, each owning one partition's `OrderedConsumer`. Since ordered consumers are ephemeral and stateless, restarts are safe — JetStream resumes from the last delivered sequence without explicit ack state management.

Avoid multiplexing one consumer across partitions: a slow message on `events.p2` won't stall `events.p3`. Cross-partition ordering is intentionally undefined. If global ordering is required, collapse to a single subject — but accept that throughput is now single-threaded. Subject design is your partition contract; get it right at schema time, not after data accumulates.

bytes.topic: Scaling — horizontal consumer scaling, partition count, rebalancing
bytes.title: NATS JetStream — Horizontal Consumer Scaling Without Partition Rebalancing
bytes.body:
JetStream pull consumers scale horizontally without coordination: spin up N instances sharing the same durable consumer name and NATS distributes pending messages across all active fetchers — no leader election, no rebalancing delay. Each `Fetch()` call atomically reserves a batch, so there's no double-delivery risk.

For explicit partitioning, shard by subject prefix: publish to `orders.{shard}` where shard is `crc32(key) % N`, then bind each consumer instance to a distinct `FilterSubject`. This gives predictable key locality — essential for ordered-per-key processing. Unlike Kafka, there's no broker-side partition metadata to rebalance when you add consumers; deploy an instance with a new `FilterSubject` and it starts consuming immediately.

To increase fan-out, attach additional durable pull consumers with disjoint `FilterSubjects` on the same stream. NATS retains the stream independently of consumer count, so late-attached consumers replay from any `DeliverPolicy` without manual offset management. Monitor lag via `nats consumer info <stream> <consumer>` or scrape the `nats_consumer_num_pending` Prometheus metric per shard.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — queue depth alerts, consumer lag, dead letter queue alerts
bytes.title: NATS JetStream — Queue Depth, Consumer Lag, and Dead Letter Alerts
bytes.body:
JetStream exposes consumer health directly via `js.ConsumerInfo("stream", "consumer")`. The returned `*nats.ConsumerInfo` carries two critical lag fields: `NumPending` (messages in the stream not yet delivered to this consumer) and `NumAckPending` (delivered but unacknowledged — in-flight). Poll these on a ticker and alert when either breaches a threshold.

For dead-letter alerting, NATS publishes advisory events to `$JS.EVENT.ADVISORY.CONSUMER.MAX_DELIVERIES.<stream>.<consumer>` whenever a message exceeds its `MaxDeliver` limit. Each advisory payload carries the stream sequence number of the poisoned message. Subscribe to this subject and route those sequences to a dedicated `DEAD_LETTER` stream via `js.Publish` in the handler.

The `/jsz?consumers=true` HTTP monitoring endpoint exposes the same fields in JSON, making it trivial to scrape with Prometheus without touching the Go client. Use both approaches together: consumer polling catches throughput degradation early; advisory subscriptions fire the moment a message is poisoned. One tells you the system is slow — the other tells you it's broken.
